focus: Resolve Q1 by comparing SQLite plus FTS5 and embeddings, a dedicated graph database, and a hybrid graph projection; determine required traversal, operational trade-offs, minimum topology, and graph-promotion conditions.
noveltyJustification: 7 of 8 findings were fully new and 1 was partially new; resolving Q1 added the 0.10 simplicity bonus, capped at 1.0.
ruledOut: Dedicated graph database in the minimum topology
Treating unresolved similar labels as canonical edges
Materializing shared facets or vector similarity as authoritative edges
sourcesQueried: command: bounded Node canonical-style relationship inventory

summary: Canonical styles contain explained similar-business candidates that the current retrieval projection omits.
Only 2269 of 5812 similar labels resolve to one canonical style name; unresolved and ambiguous targets must remain quarantined.
sources: command: bounded Node canonical-style relationship inventory

summary: Both named in-repo graph databases are normalized SQLite adjacency projections with indexed endpoints and transactional writes.
Graph query contracts are mostly one-hop SQL; multi-hop evidence/provenance paths are bounded application BFS and DAG depth.
Style relationship needs are direct similar neighbors, shared relational attributes, and vector neighbors; similarity is not transitively authoritative.
Minimum production topology is one published SQLite generation with FTS5 and normalized edges plus a profile-addressed vector projection.
Any future graph is a rebuildable generation-stamped projection with graph-down fallback, never an authority.
Graph promotion requires a measured variable-depth workload, SQLite SLO failure, relevance lift, identity-quality readiness, and projection operations.
